text: The camera provides a clear view of the bottom of the pelvis, revealing a deep pelvic cavity in the female patient.
The surgeon inserts a surgical device into the port, ensuring it is securely connected for the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon has completely separated the rich membrane, ensuring the preservation of the nervous system function during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon inserts a surgical mesh into the abdomen, using a machine arm equipped with an anti-seizure band to position the mesh accurately within the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon uses a knife to cut the thread after the needlework is completed.

The surgeon removes the thread and needle from the surgical site and introduces a new thread and needle into the body. The second needle is sewn under the hip bone, and the thread is subsequently cut and removed.
The surgeon uses their fingers to press on the muscles at the base of the pelvis and lower back, confirming the position and stabilizing the pelvis to prevent it from falling.
The surgeon removes the sewing needle and line from the hand after completing the sewing line. The third needle is then positioned on the left side of the bone and at the bottom of the muscle.
The surgeon uses a non-discriminatory sewing line to secure the webbing on the lower bone, which is visibly raised at the center of the body.

The surgeon identifies the location of the lower jaw, positioned between two large blood vessels at the tip of the head, ensuring careful manipulation to prevent damage to the vessels and avoid bleeding.
The surgeon cuts additional thread to remove the knot, followed by trimming the excess fabric from the surgical site using hand manipulation.

The surgeon inserts the V-LOOK-SARMAPS sewing needle, threading the V-LOOK sewing thread through a small hole at the end. The sewing thread appears slightly crooked, allowing it to move in one direction without needing to be tied when the seam is tight.
